rows.v: Fresh grass, steamed greens, umami, light sweetness
Japanese green teas (steamed) tend to be more vegetal and umami; Chinese green teas (pan-fired) tend to be nuttier and toastier.
Tie Guan Yin, Da Hong Pao, Milk Oolong
Dim sum, roasted nuts, light pastries
The most variable tea category — a lightly oxidized oolong can taste closer to green tea, while a heavily oxidized one can taste closer to black tea.
Assam, Darjeeling, English Breakfast, Earl Grey
The easiest tea to add milk and sugar to — its boldness holds up well to additions
Light fruit, delicate pastries, fresh cheeses
Its delicate flavor is easily overwhelmed — avoid pairing with strong or spicy foods, and never add milk.
Gin, Earl Grey tea syrup, lemon, egg white
The bergamot in Earl Grey is what makes this work — its citrus-floral oil bridges the gap between tea and gin beautifully.
Japanese whisky, iced green tea, soda water
A popular izakaya order in Japan — the tea's umami softens the whisky's edge nicely.
Ask for it "half and half" if you want to control the sweetness — some places pre-mix it sweeter than others.
Strong black tea, warming spices, steamed milk, honey
"Chai" literally means "tea" — when ordering, "chai latte" specifically means the spiced black tea version, not plain tea with milk.
White rum, matcha powder, mint, lime, soda
Matcha is powdered whole green tea leaves (not steeped and discarded), which is why it works in a shaken or blended drink rather than just brewed.
